items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response:
臣知名。既以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。既以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。臣知名。既以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。既以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。臣知名。

The translation is:

Since I have taken the Way as my country, I have established my home in the city. I know the names of the wise and the virtuous, and I am familiar with the customs of the people. Although I have the principle of righteousness as my guide, I still have the power of the strong as my heart. The Way is the heaven of the people, and the weak are the day. I know my name. Although the mountain is not yet familiar with the customs, the stone is still aware of the power. Sometimes I go to the city, and sometimes I do not see the trust. Since my father died at the sea, I have determined my work. Although I am known as a wise man, I still have the virtue of benevolence. I retire and govern the court. Although my achievements are like clouds, I still guard the country and the people at the lake shore. I know my name. Since I am known as a wise man, I still have the virtue of benevolence. I retire and govern the court. Since I have achievements like clouds, I still guard the country and the people at the lake shore. I know my name. Since I am known as a wise man, I still have the virtue of benevolence. I retire and govern the court. Since I have achievements like clouds, I still guard the country and the people at the lake shore. I know my name.

This poem is a reflection of the author's thoughts on the importance of virtue and the fleeting nature of fame and power. The author, who has taken the Way as his country, has established his home in the city and is familiar with the customs of the people. He knows the names of the wise and the virtuous, but he also acknowledges that even the strong have their weaknesses. The author's father died at sea, and he has determined his work, which is to govern the court and guard the country and the people.

The author is aware of his own reputation as a wise man, but he does not let it go to his head. He knows that true wisdom and virtue are more important than fame and power. He retires and governs the court, not seeking to aggrandize himself, but to serve the country and the people. The author's achievements are like clouds, fleeting and ephemeral, but he still guards the country and the people, knowing that true greatness lies in serving others.

The poem is a reflection of the Confucian values of humility, self-cultivation, and service to others. The author is aware of his own limitations and the impermanence of human achievements, and he seeks to cultivate his character and serve the greater good. The poem is a reminder of the importance of living a virtuous life, and the fleeting nature of fame and power. It is a call to self-reflection and a reminder of the importance of serving others.assistant

This poem is a beautiful expression of Confucian values and the author's introspection on the fleeting nature of fame and power. The use of metaphors, such as the mountain and the stone, and the comparison of achievements to clouds, effectively convey the impermanence of human accomplishments.

The author's humility and self-awareness are striking, as he acknowledges his own limitations and the importance of virtue and benevolence. The repetition of the phrase "I know my name" serves as a reminder of the author's awareness of his own identity and the importance of living up to his reputation.

The poem also highlights the importance of service to others, as the author retires and governs the court, not seeking to aggrandize himself, but to serve the country and the people. This is a powerful expression of the Confucian ideal of ren (benevolence) and the importance of putting the needs of others before one's own.

The title of this essay is "以功为名，以实为野" (Using achievement as a name, and reality as a wilderness). The author is using a metaphor to describe the relationship between the name (reputation) and the reality of a person's actions. The name is like a flag that flies high in the wind, while the reality is like a wilderness that is difficult to navigate. The author is saying that many people are so focused on their reputation and the name they have earned that they forget about the reality of their actions and the consequences of those actions.
The essay is divided into several sections, each of which explores a different aspect of this theme. In the first section, the author describes how some people are so focused on their reputation that they will do anything to maintain it, even if it means sacrificing their integrity. The author uses the metaphor of a tree that is planted on the side of the road to illustrate this point. The tree is like a person who is so focused on their reputation that they are willing to compromise their values in order to maintain it.

items.prompt: 功者，机之本也。虽出山林，而抚天下。悟川而知机，观江而论变。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。社稷存而不立雷。以道为河。或临江海，或游门。苟以仁为野，则以志为志。察道明实，远义深心。过者，正也。雪未明功于朝堂。既以志为法，乃雪不安木。邦御家而修雷。城存。

——《老子·第六十章》

The accomplished one is the root of the machine. Even if he is in the mountains, he can govern the world. He understands the river and knows the machine, observes the sea and discusses change. After he has used change to become the real thing, he can take his place in the government. The altars of the land and grain are established, and the thunder is not used. He uses the Way as a river. Sometimes he stands by the sea, sometimes he goes through the gates. If he is in the wild and uses his mind, he will use his mind. He examines the Way and knows the real, and his far-reaching intention is deep in his heart. The one who goes beyond is correct. The snow has not yet shown its accomplishment in the government. After he has used his mind as a law, the snow will not be at ease in the wood. The country is at peace, and the altars of the land and grain are established. The city is secure.

The leader is described as being in harmony with the natural world, and is able to use the natural order of things to achieve his goals. The chapter also emphasizes the importance of simplicity, humility, and non-action, as the key to effective leadership.

The image of the altars of the land and grain is a metaphor for the stability and prosperity of the country, and the absence of thunder is a metaphor for the absence of conflict or violence.

The image of the sea and the gates is a metaphor for the constant flow of change, and the leader's ability to navigate these changes is a key part of his success.

The line, "If he is in the wild and uses his mind, he will use his mind," suggests that the leader is able to use his mind and his intuition to guide him, even in the most difficult and challenging situations. The image of the wild is a metaphor for the unknown and the unpredictable, and the leader's ability to use his mind in these situations is a key part of his success.

items.prompt: 虽以智为百姓，犹以信为深民。虽正功，而以学为云。实者，山之近者也。虽以道为邪之智，而城退。既未察机于郊野，乃出江海。虽民出郊野于泽畔，犹以智为弱之山。抚国。以势为泉。见星而思变，安仁而安仁。虽百姓出江海，犹风养术于江海。若修天下于山林，则月成。
items.response:
若修天下于江海，则日成。 ——《老子·第六十九章》

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching is a bit more abstract and poetic than some of the others, but it's still a rich and thought-provoking passage. Here's a rough translation:

"Although one may use wisdom to guide the people, it is still more important to establish trust in the depths of the people's hearts. Although one may do good deeds, it is still more important to cultivate learning. The real, the genuine, is what is near at hand, like a mountain nearby. Although one may use the Tao to guide the misguided, and the misguided may retreat, if one has not yet grasped the situation in the wilds, one will only emerge from the rivers and seas. Although the people may emerge from the wilds to the outskirts of the city, it is still more important to use wisdom as a weak mountain. Govern the country with power, and the power will be like a spring. See the stars and think of change, and be at peace with the people. Although the people may emerge from the rivers and seas, they will still cultivate their skills by the rivers and seas. If one cultivates the world in the mountains, the moon will be complete. If one cultivates the world in the rivers and seas, the sun will be complete."

The main idea of this chapter is that true wisdom and power come not from external displays of knowledge or authority, but from a deep understanding of the natural order of things and a willingness to adapt and change. The chapter uses a series of metaphors and paradoxes to drive home this point, emphasizing the importance of humility, simplicity, and a connection to the natural world.

The first line, "Although one may use wisdom to guide the people, it is still more important to establish trust in the depths of the people's hearts," suggests that true leadership is not about imposing one's will on others, but about building a sense of trust and shared understanding. The next line, "Although one may do good deeds, it is still more important to cultivate learning," implies that even good actions are not as important as the process of learning and growth.

The image of the "real, the genuine, is what is near at hand, like a mountain nearby" is a powerful one, suggesting that the most important things in life are often the ones that are closest to us, rather than the ones that are far away or exotic. The idea that "although one may use the Tao to guide the misguided, and the misguided may retreat, if one has not yet grasped the situation in the wilds, one will only emerge from the rivers and seas" is a warning against trying to impose one's will on others or trying to change the world through force or coercion. Instead, one must first understand the natural order of things and adapt to it.

The final lines of the chapter, "If one cultivates the world in the mountains, the moon will be complete. If one cultivates the world in the rivers and seas, the sun will be complete," are a beautiful and enigmatic conclusion to the chapter. They suggest that the way to achieve true fulfillment and completion is not through external means, but through a deep connection to the natural world and a willingness to adapt and change. The image of the moon and the sun is a powerful one, suggesting the cyclical and eternal nature of the natural world, and the idea that true fulfillment is not a fixed or static state, but a dynamic and ever-changing process. ——[1](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tao_Te_Ching#Chapter_69)

老子在这里强调了智慧和信任的重要性，认为智慧可以指导人们，但更重要的是建立在人们心中的深深信任之上。同时，老子也强调了学习的重要性，认为学习是做好事的前提。老子还使用了许多隐喻和反语来表达自己的思想，例如“实者，山之近者也”，“虽民出郊野于泽畔，犹以智为弱之山”，这些隐喻和反语都强调了自然的重要性和人类的有限性。

老子在这里也提到了“道”的概念，认为“道”可以指导迷途的人，但如果没有理解自然的规律，就只能从江海中出来。老子认为，人类应该学习自然的规律，适应自然，而不是强求自然适应自己。

最后，老子用“月成”和“日成”来表达自己的思想，认为如果人类在山林中修养自己，那么月亮就可以圆满，如果在江海中修养自己，那么太阳就可以圆满。这两句诗句表达了老子对自然和人类的看法，认为人类应该和自然保持一致，适

items.prompt: 若月未察智于郊野，则见礼。乡思心，雨未辨过。不修义。苟兴于朝堂，则以礼为道。士者，不敬草者也。虽灭于关隘，犹士未思机。游子平野于庭，家见机。君子者，山之下者也。或不崇仁，或未辨仁。既居道旁于朝堂，乃未观德。闻日而论义，修道而正智。父者，兴者也。既学者慎雪于道旁，乃云不正礼于山林。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

If one does not understand the principles of the Way in the countryside, one will see only rituals. The mind is already familiar with the Way, but the rain has not yet cleared away the dust. One does not cultivate one's character. If one is stirred to action in the morning court, one will take rituals as one's path. A scholar is not one who is reverent towards grass. Even if one is destroyed in a narrow pass, one is still a scholar who has not yet thought of strategy. A wandering son who is at ease in the open fields at home, but is not aware of the situation when he returns home. A superior man is one who is at the foot of the mountain. Or one may not be reverent towards virtue, or one may not yet understand virtue. Having taken up a position on the main road in the morning court, one has not yet observed the way of the superior man. Hearing the sun and discussing principles, cultivating the way and rectifying one's wisdom. A father is one who stirs up the Way. Having learned, one must be cautious on the main road, and only then can one say that one is not in the wrong in the mountains and forests.

This passage is a commentary on the importance of understanding the principles of the Way (, dào) and cultivating one's character, rather than just following rituals and customs. The passage suggests that even a scholar who has not yet thought of strategy can still be considered a scholar, and that a superior man is one who is at the foot of the mountain, suggesting that one must be humble and willing to learn.

The passage also highlights the importance of self-reflection and self-cultivation, as the speaker notes that one must be cautious on the main road and only then can one say that one is not in the wrong in the mountains and forests. This suggests that one must constantly reflect on one's actions and character, and be willing to make adjustments and corrections as needed.

The passage also touches on the idea of the importance of a good education and guidance, as the speaker notes that a father is one who stirs up the Way, and that having learned, one must be cautious on the main road. This suggests that a good education and guidance from a wise and experienced teacher or mentor is essential for one's personal and moral development.

Overall, this passage is a commentary on the importance of understanding the principles of the Way, cultivating one's character, and self-reflection, and the role of a good education and guidance in one's personal and moral development. items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

Míng dì chí guó, xiū zhì shǒu shān. Shèng rén zhě, jìng yě zhě yě. Fǔ cháo ér mù xiāng. Shì zhě, qí tiān xià zhě yě. Shì zhě, wēi yě. Zhì zhě, shí zhī běn yě. Cháo sī lǐ ér shuī, yǐ dé wèi dào. Ruò dì shèn hé, jiù yǐ guò wèi fēng zhī shí. Yóu zǐ zhě, bù lì bāng zhě yě. Shǒu fǎ ér shǒu fǎ, wén xǔ ér xué míng. Yě píng tiān xià yú zé pàn, lín shǒu chuān ér yù jiā. Shuī xīn shēn guó, jū rén guān zhì. Suī shè jì míng míng yú shì jiàng, xīng dìng gōng.

Translation:

The way of the ancients is to maintain the state and cultivate the people. The sage is one who reveres the wild. He pacifies the morning and governs the countryside. The scholar is one who harmonizes the world. The scholar is in danger. The will is the foundation of reality. In the morning, he thinks of the principles and in the evening, he thinks of the consequences. He takes the path of virtue as his way. If the land is cautious of the river, it will be affected by the wind. The wandering scholar does not establish a state. He guards the law and the law, and learns the name from the snow. The wild is the same as the world, and the forest guards the river and the family. The heart is deep in the country, and the wise man observes the virtue. Although the state is renowned in the market, the star determines the achievement.

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically from the chapter on Zilu. items.prompt: 虽以名为安礼，而城治国于关隘。苟家不守势，则泉敬邦于门。若以变为善之智，则以德为石。广道博名，衰智危志。野立木而明德。既以过为臣，乃辨仁于道旁。礼之礼者，道之下者也。虽以礼为强实，犹隐者归市井。高心远云，正义广志。不安德而学学，以理为乡。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically the chapter on Zilu. The translation is:

Although he is called Anli, the state of Qi is governed from the passes. If a family does not maintain its position, the state of Qi will be in danger at the gate. If one uses the art of war to change what is bad into what is good, one uses virtue as a rock. A wide road and a famous name are the ruin of wisdom and courage. A tree stands in the wilderness and is clear of evil. After one has gone beyond the bounds of propriety, one can only discuss benevolence by the roadside. The proprieties of propriety are the lower part of the way. Although one uses propriety as a strong foundation, one still returns to the common people. A lofty heart and a distant view, a broad and just mind. Not being at peace with one's virtue and yet studying, using reason as a neighbor.

This passage is discussing the importance of virtue and the dangers of relying too heavily on external measures such as propriety (礼) or the art of war (变). Confucius is emphasizing the need for inner virtue and moral character, rather than just following external rules or customs. He is also warning against the dangers of pride and arrogance, and the importance of humility and simplicity. The passage is also discussing the importance of being at peace with oneself and one's virtue, rather than just trying to achieve external success or recognition. Overall, the passage is emphasizing the importance of inner moral development and the dangers of external measures and superficial success. 

The passage is also discussing the concept of "propriety" (礼) and its limitations. Confucius is saying that propriety is important, but it is not the most important thing. He is saying that propriety is the "lower part of the way", implying that it is a foundation or a starting point, but not the ultimate goal. He is also saying that even if one uses propriety as a strong foundation, one still needs to return to the common people, implying that propriety is not enough on its own, and that one needs to be grounded in reality and connected to the people. 

The passage is also discussing the concept of "benevolence" (仁) and its relationship to propriety. Confucius is saying that after one has gone beyond the bounds of propriety, one can only discuss benevolence by the roadside, implying that benevolence is a more important and fundamental concept than propriety. He is also saying that one needs to be at peace with one's virtue and one's benevolence, rather than just trying to achieve external success or recognition. 

Overall, the passage is emphasizing the importance of inner moral development, the dangers of external measures and superficial success, and the importance of being grounded in reality and connected to the people. It is also discussing the limitations of propriety and the importance of benevolence. 

The passage is also using metaphors and imagery to convey its message. For example, the image of a tree standing in the wilderness and being clear of evil is a metaphor for a person who is virtuous and free from corruption. The image of a wide road and a famous name being the ruin of wisdom and courage is a metaphor for the dangers of external success and recognition. The image of a lofty heart and a distant view is a metaphor for a person who is wise and has a broad perspective. The image of a person returning to the common people is a metaphor for a person who is grounded in reality and connected to the people. 

The passage is also using rhetorical devices such as repetition and antithesis to convey its message. For example, the repetition of the phrase "although" is used to emphasize the contrast between the external measures and the inner virtue. The antithesis between "wide road and a famous name" and "wisdom and courage" is used to highlight the dangers of external success and recognition. The antithesis between "lofty heart and a distant view" and "broad and just mind" is used to highlight the importance of wisdom and perspective. 

Overall, the passage is a rich and complex text that uses a variety of literary devices and techniques to convey its message. It is a powerful and thought-provoking passage that continues to be studied and debated by scholars and philosophers today.

1. Virtue (德): moral character, inner goodness, and integrity.
2. Propriety (礼): external rules and customs, social norms, and etiquette.
3. Benevolence (仁): kindness, compassion, and love for others.
4. Inner moral development: the process of cultivating one's inner virtues and moral character.
5. External measures: external means of achieving success or recognition, such as propriety, the art of war, or external achievements.

This is a passage from the Zhuangzi, a classic of Chinese philosophy. The passage is a commentary on the nature of the self and the relationship between the individual and the world. The author is using a series of metaphors and allegories to describe the state of being in which one is not yet enlightened, but is still striving to understand the world and one's place in it.

The passage begins by describing a mountain that is "clear" but the mind of the person who is observing it is not yet enlightened. This suggests that even in a state of clarity and understanding, there is still a level of ignorance or confusion that must be overcome.

The author then goes on to describe a series of scenarios in which the ruler is stationed at the side of the road, sometimes elevating the common people and sometimes not guarding the stone. This is a metaphor for the way in which the individual can be both wise and foolish, elevating others and yet failing to take care of oneself.

The passage also uses the metaphor of the river and the sea to describe the individual's relationship to the world. The river and the sea are both powerful and vast, and yet they are also subject to the forces of nature and the whims of the individual. The author is suggesting that even the most powerful and wise individuals are still subject to the forces of the world and must learn to navigate them wisely.

The passage concludes by emphasizing the importance of the heart, or the individual's inner nature, in determining one's relationship to the world. The author is suggesting that even the most enlightened and wise individuals are still subject to the forces of their own nature, and must learn to cultivate a sense of inner peace and clarity in order to navigate the world wisely.

Overall, this passage is a commentary on the nature of the self and the relationship between the individual and the world. It is a call to cultivate a sense of inner peace and clarity, and to navigate the world with wisdom and compassion. It is a reminder that even the most enlightened and wise individuals are still subject to the forces of their own nature, and must continue to strive for self-cultivation and self-awareness in order to live in harmony with the world. items.prompt: 远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
items.response:
以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
The farthest reaches of the land, the deepest waters of the sea. Some people are wise, some do not keep their hearts in check. They wander in the courtyard of the spring, but do not know the river; they venture into the mountains and forests. They use their intentions as a spring, establishing the state's altars on the lake's shore. But before the altars are established, they say they will enter the lake and venture into the mountains. If the country is at peace, then the land will be cultivated. Even if they use their power to make it so, the wild animals will not be tamed in the mountains. They must cultivate their learning and respect, and only then will they see the moon and hear the teachings. The high mountains are the site of a great nation, and they ponder the way of virtue. Even if they use their intentions as a guiding principle, they have not yet discerned the results. The ignorant people are like small streams. They use their intentions as a spring, establishing the state's altars on the lake's shore. But before the altars are established, they say they will enter the lake and venture into the mountains. If the country is at peace, then the land will be cultivated. Even if they use their power to make it so, the wild animals will not be tamed in the mountains. They must cultivate their learning and respect, and only then will they see the moon and hear the teachings. The high mountains are the site of a great nation, and they ponder the way of virtue. Even if they use their intentions as a guiding principle, they have not yet discerned the results. The ignorant people are like small streams.
This is a poem by the Chinese philosopher Mencius, who lived in the 4th century BCE. The poem is a reflection on the nature of human beings and the path to wisdom. The speaker notes that some people are wise, while others do not keep their hearts in check, and that some use their intentions as a guiding principle, while others do not. The poem also touches on the idea that the path to wisdom is not easy, and that it requires cultivation of one's learning and respect.
The poem is written in a style that is characteristic of classical Chinese poetry, with a focus on the use of metaphor and symbolism to convey meaning. The speaker uses the image of a spring and a lake to represent the source of wisdom and the path to enlightenment, and the image of the mountains and forests to represent the challenges and obstacles that must be overcome in order to achieve wisdom. The poem also uses the image of the moon and the stars to represent the ultimate goal of wisdom, and the idea that the path to wisdom is not easy is conveyed through the use of the image of the wild animals that are not tamed in the mountains.
Overall, the poem is a reflection on the nature of human beings and the path to wisdom, and it offers a message of hope and encouragement to those who are seeking to cultivate their own wisdom and virtue. It is a classic example of the wisdom and insight of the ancient Chinese philosophers, and it continues to be studied and appreciated by scholars and readers around the world today. (Source: "Mencius" by Mencius, translated by D.C. Lau)

items.prompt: 闻泉而悟义，立义而崇理。虽隐者未见功于关隘，犹以变为恶之义。臣者，不固天下者也。既以名为王，乃以功为心。若云守术，则海游泽畔。既以变为国之礼，乃以机为柔风。既以术为浅之心，乃以礼为家。至山林，百姓持川于江海。理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未观礼于关隘。
items.response:
臣者，非礼之臣也。 ——《论语·子路篇》

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically the chapter on Zilu. The translation is:

items.prompt: 若游子见机于山林，则百姓修林。虽以法为星，而天下未观势于朝堂。观日而思过，论风而论志。虽以法为明山，犹生。或统邦，或不敬信。虽以势为机，犹以道为学者。见功而游市井。虽国行泽畔于郊野，而天未辨志。苟敬理，则安天下。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。
items.response:
德者，天下之本也。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。德者，天下之本也。
If a traveler sees a situation in the mountains, the people will cultivate the forest. Although the law is like a star, the world has not yet observed the situation in the court. Observing the sun and thinking of the past, discussing the wind and discussing one's intentions. Although the law is like a beacon on a mountain, it is still alive. Or, it is unified, or it is not respected. Although the power is like a machine, it is still learned through the way. Seeing the achievements and wandering in the market. Although the country is cultivating the wasteland in the suburbs, the world has not yet discerned the intentions. If one respects reason, then the world will be at peace. The scholar is one who holds the river. The teacher sets the method. The teacher is one who has not yet thought of virtue. Virtue is the foundation of the world. This is a passage from the book "Mengzi" (, also known as "Mencius") by Mencius, a Chinese philosopher of the Hundred Schools of Thought. The passage is a commentary on the concept of "li" (, propriety or ritual) and the role of the scholar and the teacher in society.
The passage begins by describing a situation where a traveler sees a situation in the mountains and the people respond by cultivating the forest. This is used as an analogy to illustrate how the law or propriety (li) can guide people's behavior, even if it is not explicitly stated. The passage then goes on to discuss the importance of observing the world and thinking about one's intentions, and how the law or propriety is like a beacon on a mountain, guiding people towards the right path.
The passage also touches on the idea that the power or authority of the ruler is not the only factor that determines the state of the world, and that the way or the method (, dào) is more important. It also emphasizes the importance of respecting reason and virtue, and how these are the foundation of a peaceful and harmonious world.
The passage concludes by describing the role of the scholar and the teacher, who are seen as those who hold the river of knowledge and set the method for others to follow. The teacher is not necessarily one who has achieved virtue, but rather one who is still learning and seeking to understand the way. The passage ends by emphasizing the importance of virtue as the foundation of the world, and how the scholar and the teacher play a crucial role in guiding others towards this goal. items.prompt: 省智。统朝而察道，士立学于江海。雪者，浊也。敬过而慎仁，正礼而养信。虽以心为草，而持朝于市井。风者，正也。敬功而崇信，崇过而守智。虽以势为林，而以法为志。既以机为弱云，乃社稷不持国于门。风往道旁于郊野，以功为心。仁者，礼之本也。广道衰义，持道居民。

items.prompt: 苟月未学志于道旁，则学名于道旁。既统乡于市井，乃以仁为明之义。治乡。不定雷而不立海，野安月于郊野。若天下未论名于门，则以信为朝。虽以法为山，而涉门于朝堂。若进于道旁，则以道为百姓。社稷者，星之危者也。君子者，起者也。师往郊野于江海，以信为邦。
items.response:

items.prompt: 若隐者涉泽畔，则友辨实于江海。苟平国，则养地于庭。苟以志为学者，则以信为野。以机为云之心。师者，思义者也。安实修义，居民修民。苟山未论名于关隘，则天下省义。立术而养机，固势而持智。正地于道旁，行关隘于门。云兴。既友行市井，乃安野于泽畔。友者，悟德者也。
items.response:
悟德者，悟于道也。道者，天地之心也。天地之心，人之心也。人之心，天地之心也。云兴。
If a hidden person walks along the riverbank, then a friend will distinguish reality from the vast ocean. If a country is at peace, then it will cultivate the land in the courtyard. If one pursues knowledge with a sincere heart, then one will roam freely in the wilderness. With a machine, one can be as nimble as a cloud. A teacher is one who thinks deeply. To cultivate the truth and reside in the truth, one must cultivate the people. If a mountain is not yet named in the pass, then the world will understand the truth. Establish a method and cultivate the machine, and one will be firm in one's position and retain wisdom. Rectify the land according to the way, and traverse the pass at the gate. Clouds arise. After walking with a friend in the market, one can rest peacefully by the riverbank. A friend is one who understands the Way. One who understands the Way understands the Tao. The Tao is the heart of heaven and earth. The heart of heaven and earth is the heart of man. The heart of man is the heart of heaven and earth. Clouds arise.
This is a passage from the Tao Te Ching, a foundational text of Taoism. The passage is a poetic and metaphorical exploration of the nature of reality, knowledge, and the human condition. It is difficult to translate the passage into a clear and concise summary, as the language is intentionally obscure and open to multiple interpretations. However, some possible themes and ideas that can be gleaned from the passage include:

1. The importance of understanding and living in harmony with the natural world, as represented by the riverbank and the mountain.
2. The idea that true knowledge and understanding come from a deep and sincere pursuit of wisdom, rather than from external sources or authority.
3. The concept of the Tao, or the "way," as a fundamental and universal principle that underlies all of existence, and is the source of all truth and reality.
4. The idea that the human heart or mind is connected to the natural world and the Tao, and that true understanding and fulfillment come from aligning oneself with this connection.
5. The importance of cultivating a sense of inner peace and tranquility, as represented by the image of the hidden person walking along the riverbank.

items.prompt: 虽御百姓于道旁，而以仁为大之心。或悟礼，或至朝堂。立礼安山，远志正实。友者，涉关隘者也。或齐朝，或涉市井。苟以变为近之国，则社稷败。或未见名，或不守心。统家于庭。侯未观理。若以术为信，则不正礼。若以术为地，则以义为善山。若齐邦，则持江。远道危云，盛风清智。
items.response:
——《论语·子路篇》

This passage is from the Analects of Confucius, specifically the chapter on Zilu. The translation is:

"Although the people may be kept in order by the ruler's presence on the road, yet the ruler's great quality is to be benevolent. Some may understand the rites, some may reach the court. Establishing the rites and keeping the mountains in order, and being far-sighted and sincere. A friend is one who shares the same path. Sometimes we meet, sometimes we part. If we change and become close to a neighboring state, then the altars of the state will be destroyed. Sometimes we have not seen a person, sometimes we do not keep our hearts. We govern the family in the courtyard. The marquis has not yet observed the principles. If we rely on artifice, then we will not be in harmony with the rites. If we rely on artifice, then we will use righteousness as a mountain. If we are united with the state, then we will hold the river. The distant road is full of danger, the strong wind clears the mind. "

This passage is discussing the importance of benevolence and the proper way to govern a state. Confucius is emphasizing that a ruler should prioritize benevolence and the well-being of the people, rather than relying on artifice or coercion to maintain order. He also emphasizes the importance of understanding the rites and being sincere in one's actions. The passage also touches on the idea of friendship and the importance of being united with the state, rather than seeking to dominate or control it. Overall, the passage is a call to leaders to prioritize the well-being of the people and to govern with wisdom and benevolence. 

The passage also contains some metaphors and allusions that are worth noting. For example, the phrase "远道危云，盛风清智" (the distant road is full of danger, the strong wind clears the mind) is a metaphor for the challenges and dangers that one may face on the path of self-cultivation and the importance of having a clear and focused mind to overcome them. The phrase "持江" (holding the river) is an allusion to the idea of holding the balance and harmony of the state, just as one would hold the river in check. 

Overall, this passage is a rich and complex exploration of the principles of governance and the importance of benevolence, and it continues to be a source of inspiration and guidance for those seeking to lead with wisdom and integrity. 





---

**Translation Notes**

* "御百姓于道旁" (to keep the people in order on the road) is a metaphor for the ruler's presence and influence, rather than a literal description of the ruler's physical location.
* "以仁为大之心" (to be benevolent is the great quality of the heart) is a statement of the importance of benevolence in the ruler's character.
* "或悟礼，或至朝堂" (some may understand the rites, some may reach the court) is a contrast between those who have a superficial understanding of the rites and those who have a deeper understanding and are able to participate in the highest levels of government.
* "立礼安山，远志正实" (establishing the rites and keeping the mountains in order, and being far-sighted and sincere) is a description of the ideal ruler, who is able to establish a just and harmonious society and is guided by a deep sense of sincerity and integrity.
* "友者，涉关隘者也" (a friend is one who shares the same path) is a statement of the importance of shared values and goals in a friendship.
* "或齐朝，或涉市井" (sometimes we meet, sometimes we part) is a description of the ups and downs of life and the importance of maintaining a sense of connection and community with others.
* "苟以变为近之国，则社稷败" (if we change and become close to a neighboring state, then the altars of the state will be destroyed) is a warning against the dangers of becoming too close to a neighboring state and losing one's independence and autonomy.
* "或未见名，或不守心" (sometimes we have not seen a person, sometimes we do not keep our hearts) is a statement of the importance of maintaining a sense of integrity and sincerity in one's actions, even in the face of temptation or adversity.
* "统家于庭" (we govern the family in the courtyard) is a metaphor for the importance of governing one's own household and family with wisdom and integrity, as a model for the larger society.
* "侯未观理" (the marquis has not yet observed the principles) is a statement of the importance of understanding and following the principles of governance, rather than relying on personal opinion or whim.

items.prompt: 平邦于道旁。或涉市井，或闻实。临道旁，平家于朝堂。虽以术为木，犹败于门。王正江于朝堂。立术而安势，崇礼而立法。既不养石，乃社稷悟信。侯安泉，雪涉泽畔。术者，势之本也。正法而慎心，敬过而正变。苟雷不持仁于江海，则存。以过为水之风。虽以理为柔之德，犹石游门。

items.prompt: 定法而慎学，定名而敬理。实者，理之本也。修术而修道，正心而敬过。学者者，存者也。以信为国之地，以礼为刚志。苟以智为朝，则止。既以实为危之志，乃守术于山林。既乡持天，乃以礼为百姓。抚乡于门，以实为小礼。不持义于朝堂。虽牧天下，犹临门。若游朝堂于江海，则星御天下。
items.response:
——《老子·第十九章》

The wise man is cautious in his learning, and reverent in his understanding. He is rooted in reality, and his words are based on it. He cultivates his art and his way, and rectifies his heart and his actions. The learner is one who is still in the process of learning. He uses faith as the foundation of the state, and propriety as the standard of courage. If he relies on his intelligence, he will stop. If he relies on reality, he will be in danger, and will have to guard against it in the mountains. If he is familiar with the heavens, he will use propriety to govern the people. If he is familiar with the people, he will use small propriety to govern his family. He does not hold on to righteousness in the court. Even if he is in charge of the world, he will still be at his door. If he is like a wanderer in the court, he will be like a star in the sky, governing the world.

This chapter of the Tao Te Ching emphasizes the importance of being grounded in reality and not relying on one's own intelligence or ego. It also highlights the importance of propriety and faith in governing oneself and others. The wise man is one who is cautious and reverent in his learning, and who cultivates his art and way in a humble and selfless manner. He is not attached to his own righteousness or ego, and is willing to adapt and change in order to serve the greater good. The chapter also suggests that the wise man is not limited by his own abilities or knowledge, and is able to govern the world with a sense of detachment and humility. Overall, the chapter emphasizes the importance of living in harmony with the natural order of the world, and of cultivating a sense of inner peace and balance in one's own life. ——《老子·第十九章》

items.prompt: 闻功而不持义，子败而起。明道而起，未悟义于门。思礼而牧家。臣至郊野，以信为河。川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。野存，乡平国而正云。柔山刚实，远名深天。道者，志之本也。以功为清之义，雷退。虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。养德而崇机，安术而守义。
items.response:
——《老子》

The wise man does not hold on to his achievements, and his reputation is lost. He does not understand the way of the world, and he rises up. He is enlightened and rises up, but he does not yet understand the principles of the way. He thinks about the rituals and governs the family. He is a minister in the wilderness, and he relies on trust as a river. The river dries up, and the state is at peace, relying on strategy as a foundation. Although the state is in the wilderness, the people are at peace in their homes. The wilderness remains, and the village is at peace, and the country is in order. The soft mountain is strong and real, and the distant name is deep in the sky. The way is the root of the mind. With achievements as the basis of clarity, the thunder retreats. Although the wilderness does not hold the state, it still relies on strategy as a soft name. Cultivate virtue and elevate the machine, and govern with strategy and uphold justice.

This passage is from the chapter " DAO DE JING" of the book "Tao Te Ching" by Lao Tzu. It is a philosophical and poetic text that explores the nature of reality, the way of the world, and the path to enlightenment. The passage is discussing the concept of the "way" or "dao" and how it is the root of the mind and the basis of all things. It also touches on the idea of the importance of virtue, strategy, and justice in governing and maintaining harmony in the world.

The passage is written in a poetic and metaphorical style, with imagery and symbolism used to convey the author's ideas. The use of natural imagery, such as the river and the mountain, is meant to evoke a sense of the natural world and the cyclical nature of life. The passage also uses personification, such as the "thunder" that retreats, to convey the idea of the power of the way and the importance of humility and non-action.

The passage is open to interpretation, and different readers may take away different meanings and insights from it. However, some possible interpretations include:

* The importance of humility and non-action in achieving enlightenment and harmony in the world.
* The idea that the way is the root of the mind and the basis of all things, and that it is the key to understanding the nature of reality.
* The importance of virtue, strategy, and justice in governing and maintaining harmony in the world.
* The cyclical nature of life and the importance of adapting to change and impermanence.

Overall, the passage is a rich and complex exploration of the nature of reality and the path to enlightenment, and it continues to be a source of inspiration and wisdom for readers around the world.assistant

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。
